senses.definition: a genus of water mold that resembles fungi, known for causing disease in fish eggs and fish tissue
examples: Saprolegnias can infect fish eggs in freshwater.
Researchers studied the role of saprolegnias in aquaculture disease outbreaks.

senses.definition: relating to or caused by organisms of the genus Saprolegnia; mold-like in appearance or behavior
examples: Saprolegnious infections affect fish health in hatcheries.
The saprolegnious outbreak led to significant fry mortality.

senses.definition: a soft, weathered rock layer derived from weathered feldspar minerals, usually forming a clay-rich surface layer
examples: The miners explored saprolite zones rich in laterite soils.
Saprolite forms a weathered mantle over bedrock in tropical soils.

senses.definition: plural of saprolite; soft, weathered rock layers formed by chemical breakdown of minerals
examples: The region contains extensive saprolites that overlie bedrock.
Metal ore exploration often targets saprolites due to mineral enrichment.

senses.definition: relating to or consisting of saprolite; chemically weathered rock material
examples: Saprolitic soils are rich in clay minerals.
The saprolitic layer is often set above solid bedrock.

senses.definition: relating to sapropy otherwise unclear; likely incorrect or extremely rare variation in scholarly contexts; not widely used
examples: In rare texts, sapromic appears as an obsolete or erroneous form.

senses.definition: an organic-rich, fine-grained sediment formed in stagnant water and often used as a soil conditioner
examples: The farmer added sapropel to the fields to improve fertility.
Sapropel deposits can indicate historical wetland conditions.

senses.definition: of or containing sapropel; relating to sapropels
examples: Sapropelic sediments indicate ancient anoxic conditions.
Scientists studied sapropelic layers for paleoenvironment research.

senses.definition: a lithified sapropel; a rock composed of organic-rich sediment
examples: The sapropelite sample revealed substantial preserved organic matter.
Sapropelite formations are studied in petroleum geology.

senses.definition: plural of sapropelite; rocks formed from sapropel-rich materials
examples: The area contains several sapropelite-bearing formations.
Explorers collected sapropelite samples from the river delta.

senses.definition: a fine, organic-rich sediment deposited in still water, often used in soil improvement and palynology
examples: Sapropels form in lakes with low oxygen and high organic content.
Palynologists study sapropels to infer ancient climates.

senses.definition: an organism that feeds on decaying organic matter; a saprophage
examples: Many saprophagan organisms help recycle nutrients in ecosystems.
The study focused on saprophagan species in forest litter.

senses.definition: feeding on decaying organic matter; saprophagous organisms decompose dead material
examples: Earthworms are saprophagous creatures in soil ecosystems.
Saprophagous bacteria contribute to nutrient cycling.

senses.definition: an organism that feeds on or derives nutrients from decaying organic matter; a saprophage
examples: Some fungi are saprophiles, living on dead plant material.
The saprophile decomposers accelerate soil formation.

senses.definition: relating to saprophiles or saprophilous organisms; feeding on decaying matter
examples: Saprophilous fungi are common in leaf litter.
The study described saprophilous communities in the compost pile.

senses.definition: an organism that feeds on dead or decaying organic matter; a saprophytic organism
examples: Many saprophytes play a key role in decomposition.
Fungi are common saprophytes in forest ecosystems.

senses.definition: plural of saprophyte; organisms that feed on dead or decaying organic matter
examples: Saprophytes break down dead plant material in the litter layer.
Fungi and some bacteria are saprophytes.

senses.definition: relating to saprophytes or their mode of obtaining nourishment from decaying matter
examples: Saprophytic fungi help recycle nutrients in forests.
The lab studied saprophytic enzymes in decomposition.

senses.definition: in a saprophytic manner; by feeding on or deriving nutrients from decaying matter
examples: The fungus colonies grew saprophytically on the dead wood.
Saprophytically acting organisms contribute to nutrient cycling.

senses.definition: the condition or practice of deriving nourishment from decaying matter; saprophytism as a biological phenomenon
examples: Saprophytism is common among many fungi.
Researchers studied the ecological role of saprophytism in soil.

senses.definition: plural of saprophytism; instances or forms of saprophytism
examples: Various saprophytisms have evolved in different fungal lineages.
The textbook compares different saprophytisms in soils.

senses.definition: a small planktonic organism that lives in water with decaying organic matter; a saproplankton
examples: Saproplankton plays a role in the marine detrital food web.
Researchers sampled saproplankton to study nutrient cycling.

senses.definition: capable of opening or fitting through a sapro-stomous feature; related to sapro-stomous anatomy (rare/technical term)
examples: The specimen exhibited saprostomous features in its mouthparts.

senses.definition: an organism that feeds on or derives nutrients from dead organic matter; a saprotroph
examples: Fungi that break down fallen leaves are saprotrophs.
Soil ecosystems rely on saprotrophs for nutrient release.

senses.definition: relating to saprotrophs or their mode of feeding on decaying matter
examples: Saprotrophic fungi contribute to the breakdown of organic litter.
The saprotrophic activity releases nutrients into the soil.
